slides.title: The Big Three: OS Ecosystems
slides.content:
Operating Systems (OS) manage hardware and software. 
- **Linux**: The engine of the web (Servers, Android, Docker).
- **Windows**: The backbone of Enterprise and Gaming.
- **macOS**: Unix-based polish for creative and dev workflows.

slides.title: Mobile Giants: Android & iOS
slides.content:
Smartphones follow the same logic as desktops but optimized for touch and power.
- **Android**: Linux-based, open ecosystem, Java/Kotlin primary.
- **iOS**: XNU-based (Unix), closed garden, Swift/Obj-C primary.

slides.title: The Hardware Trinity: CPU, RAM, Disk
slides.content:
Computers process data in cycles:
- **CPU (Central Processing Unit)**: The 'Brain' executing instructions.
- **RAM (Random Access Memory)**: Fast, volatile 'Desk Space' for active tasks.
- **Storage (HDD/SSD)**: 'Warehouse' for persistent data and code.

slides.title: Persistence & The Hard Disk
slides.content: Unlike RAM, hard disks are **non-volatile**. Even when power is lost, data remains. In modern engineering, we optimize for **I/O (Input/Output)** speeds, where SSDs (Solid State Drives) outperform rotating HDDs.

slides.title: The Command Line Interface (CLI)
slides.content: The Terminal is the text-based entrance to the OS. It is faster and more powerful for automation than a GUI. Common commands include `ls` (list), `cd` (change directory), `mkdir` (make directory).

slides.title: Pipes & Redirection: The Unix Philosophy
slides.content:
One of the most powerful CLI concepts is the **Pipe (`|`)**. It takes the output of one command and sends it as input to another. 
- `grep` searches text.
- `ps aux | grep node` finds running Node processes.

slides.title: Processes vs. Threads
slides.content:
- **Process**: An instance of a program in execution (isolated memory).
- **Thread**: A 'sub-task' within a process (shared memory). 
Multithreading allows a program to do multiple things at once (e.g., UI and background sync).

slides.title: Memory Management & Virtual RAM
slides.content: The OS manages RAM so programs don't crash into each other. **Virtual Memory** uses disk space to simulate more RAM than physically available (Paging/Swapping).

slides.title: I/O Redirection: Save Your Output
slides.content:
Use `>` to overwrite a file with command output, and `>>` to append to it. 
Example: `echo 'Hello' > hello.txt` creates a file. `ls >> file_list.txt` saves your current directory state.

slides.title: Filesystem & File Descriptors
slides.content:
In Unix/Linux, **'Everything is a file'**. A **File Descriptor (FD)** is an integer representing an open file, socket, or pipe. Standard FDs: 
- `0`: stdin (Keyboard)
- `1`: stdout (Screen)
- `2`: stderr (Errors)

slides.title: Networking: The OSI 7-Layer Model
slides.content:
Data travels in layers, from physical wires to application code. 
- **L3 (Network)**: Routing (IP).
- **L4 (Transport)**: Reliable delivery (TCP/UDP).
- **L7 (Application)**: Logic (HTTP, SSH, DNS).

slides.title: TCP vs. UDP: Reliability vs. Speed
slides.content:
- **TCP (Transmission Control Protocol)**: Handshakes, checks for missing data. Used for Web (HTTP), Email, SSH.
- **UDP (User Datagram Protocol)**: Sends data and forgets. Used for Streaming, Gaming, Voice Calls.

slides.title: Ports: The Logical Doors
slides.content:
Think of an IP as a building and a **Port** as an apartment number. 
- **80/443**: HTTP/HTTPS (Web).
- **22**: SSH (Remote Access).
- **8080/3000**: Common Dev Server ports.

slides.title: SSH: Secure Shell
slides.content: SSH is the standard protocol for managing servers remotely. It uses asymmetric encryption. `ssh user@host` allows you to open a terminal on a machine halfway across the world securely.

slides.title: DNS: The Phonebook of the Internet
slides.content: Computers communicate via IPs (e.g., `142.250.190.46`), but humans use names (`google.com`). **DNS (Domain Name System)** translates names into IP addresses.
